methods.description: This method involves measuring the intrusion pressure of oil in a diatomite membrane to evaluate its potential for separating oil/water mixtures. The theoretical intrusion pressure is calculated using the oil/water interfacial tension, oil contact angle underwater, and pore radius. The experimentally measured intrusion pressure is compared to the theoretical value to assess the membrane's performance.

steps.description: Calculate the theoretical intrusion pressure using the formula P_theoretical = (2 * γ_OW * cosθ_OW) / r, where γ_OW is the oil/water interfacial tension, θ_OW is the oil contact angle underwater, and r is the pore radius.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate theoretical intrusion pressure using interfacial tension, contact angle, and pore radius

steps.description: Compare the experimentally measured intrusion pressure with the theoretical value to evaluate the membrane's performance.
steps.searchable_summary: compare experimental and theoretical intrusion pressures

steps.description: Analyze the structural complexity of the diatomite membrane, including micro-sized channels and submicro/nano-scaled roughness and pores, to understand why the experimentally measured intrusion pressure is higher than the theoretical value.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze membrane structure to explain pressure discrepancy
outputs: explanation for higher experimental intrusion pressure
